One of our favorite places to go in New York! ||| [1★ yashomati mukherjee]: This review honestly breaks my heart to write. The Headless Widow has always been one of our favorite bars in NYC. We are regulars, we’ve recommended it to friends and family countless times, and it has consistently been one of our top suggestions when people ask where to go. We love the ambience, the drinks, and the overall vibe of the place — which is exactly why last night’s experience was so shocking and disappointing.
When my husband and I walked in, there were tables open. The bar itself seemed mostly full, but the small wooden tables by the window with barstools were almost completely empty. We told the server who greeted us that we were hoping to sit either at the bar or at one of those window barstools. He told us we could stand at the bar to order, which we agreed to.
While grabbing menus from the bar, we noticed that only one woman was sitting at the window barstools — all the other stools were empty. I spoke to her, and she kindly told us she was about to leave anyway because her friends were running late and she had another reservation. She said we were welcome to take her spot.
I sat down, and my husband stayed by the bar to look at the menu. Almost immediately, the same server came over and told us we couldn’t sit there and that we had to stand at the bar. At this point, the woman who had been sitting there stepped in and explained to him that she was leaving and that we had spoken to her. She even seemed confused herself, saying she didn’t understand why it was an issue since she was getting up and no one else was sitting there. At that point, I told my husband that I no longer felt welcome, and we decided to leave. It completely ruined our night and left a really bitter taste — not just because of the situation, but because this was a place we genuinely loved and felt connected to.
It’s sad to walk away from an establishment you’ve supported for years, but feeling unwelcome and disrespected is not something we’re willing to overlook. I hope management takes this seriously and provides better training to their staff so guests are treated with basic respect and kindness. Unfortunately, after this experience, we don’t think we’ll be returning.

Update - Thank you so much for your gracious offer and kind words. Really appreciate you taking the time out and responding to my review.  We would love to come back as paying customers like we always have but do appreciate the offer to get a table by the window. I can reach out to you on your other social media handle to maybe pick up a date. Thank you once again for restoring my faith in The Headless Widow.

The jukebox is exquisite, but last Sunday was a Josh Styles and one of the owners, Jonathan Toubin - a NYC rock n’ roll institution, with a collection of 45s so exquisitely pulled, I was suspended in fun laughs good time mode from 10pm to last call.

This is a bar for the savvy and layman alike - and honestly feels like a neightborhood joint befitting its punk rock mythology history. Jonathan Toubin (and friends Jesse Malin and Johnny T) are stalwarts and genuinely care about catering to the community, and making happenings happen. Plus the bathrooms are clean, with tp and a mirror. I couldn’t be happier.

The drink list is full of stirred, boozy options. If you like amaro, you’re in the right spot. Their cocktails lean herbal, bitter, and layered rather than sweet or flashy. They rotate drinks often, but the style stays consistent: strong, clean, and built for people who actually enjoy tasting the base spirits.

The space is small, so expect to be close to other people or even standing. The bartenders are dialed in, focused, and efficient without putting on a show. google_reviews:
[1★ Naomi Abiara]: I hardly ever write reviews and this is one of the first times where I actually feel compelled to write one because I feel it’s necessary.

I genuinely want to give Kingston Hall five stars. My friends and I loved the vibe of this sexy, tucked-away bar. It’s very dark (keep this in mind), moody, with a beautiful interior and a great bartender.

We ordered some shots and the infamous Drunken Coconut, which is served in a coconut. We thought the coconut was such a fun idea that we decided to keep it as a souvenir for laughs.

Cut to us 45 minutes later that night, stopping for fro-yo in a well-lit shop. And that’s when we saw it… Remember how I mentioned how dark the bar is? Well, turns out it was so dark that we didn’t notice the MOLD SPORES covering the entire inside of the coconut (photo attached). It looked like a full-blown ecosystem was growing in there. We were completely shocked — honestly, horrified — realizing we’d just been drinking out of something that moldy.

If we hadn’t kept the coconut, we would have never known. Which means plenty of other people probably don’t either.

All that to say: it’s very unsafe to eat or drink from here until this is addressed. I’d love to believe this was just an unfortunate oversight (a massive one) because of how dimly lit it is, but it’s still unacceptable. I really hope Kingston Hall investigates this immediately — because no one should have to worry about getting sick from a cocktail. ||| [1★ Lysol Records]: I am normally someone who does not write Google reviews, but my experience here was so horrendous that I feel extremely compelled to leave a review. This past Saturday night, they not only double-booked, but TRIPLE-BOOKED themselves (Google Calendar is free?). I wouldn't have left this review if the staff and management at Kingston handled the situation properly, but instead, they handled it extremely unprofessionally and poorly. They only honored one party in the private space (no clue how they decided they got the space, and there were probably at most 10 people in this party), and the multiple other parties who were screwed over for the night were packed so tightly at the bar that it was basically a fire hazard. Their bouncer played dumb, and their manager "was not in tonight." It was basically handled as a "too bad, sucks to suck" situation. Additionally, whoever plans their events does not answer emails because they are cowards. Conclusion: do not come here; go to the 100 other better bars in NYC that actually care about their patrons that make simple accommodations when they screw up. Mistakes happen; it is just all about how you rectify the situation. It isn't brain surgery to problem-solve when you screw up, but clearly this place lacks the skills and training to do so. ||| [5★ Hermes Arriola]: I can’t really speak to food because I don’t know if they had it or not. Last night I attended the Danity Kane concert. There were 3 types of tickets for this show, VIP With meet and greet which were let in first, Reg VIP and GA. We went through metal detectors and they checked bags. We then went to the Coat check area and bathrooms and then the bar. This is where things get messy. I feel like the people who had the reg VIP should have been let into the area where the stage was to then get drinks at that bar. Especially since we had lanyards and wrist bands to show we were Reg VIP and there was a bar upstairs with the stage was. Not only would we be able to secure a spot early and close, we would actually feel like VIP. This caused a mess because there were people with VIP meet and greet already there that were tall and right in front and people with GA upset that the tall people were blocking their view. I saw a GA girl pour a drink on the back of a VIP girl with those tall men and a fight almost happened. Luckily, security got the complaints and took the GA girls away but it created an entitlement and mess because we were all blended together.

All in all the venue is nice, the staff here is efficient and def gets their job done. The security guard was assertive and got the GA girls out of there quick and the staff at the bar were so nice. What stood out most was the bartender’s behavior toward the end. Despite me being clearly drunk, he offered me another shot, which didn’t feel appropriate. To top it off, he selected a 25% tip option for us at checkout without asking, which seemed unnecessary and pushy.

Unfortunately, because of these issues, my friend and I don’t plan on coming back.

google_reviews:
[5★ 是神户牛肉]: Bar GOTO is a refined Japanese-style cocktail bar tucked away in the Lower East Side, offering a quietly impressive and thoughtfully executed drinking experience.

The bar earned international recognition in 2022 and is led by Kenta Goto, a bartender trained at Pegu Club. His approach reinterprets modern cocktails through a Japanese lens, emphasizing balance, restraint, and ingredient logic rather than excess or spectacle.

From the moment you step inside, the warm amber lighting and orderly, intimate space create a calm and unhurried atmosphere. The environment feels precise yet welcoming, encouraging guests to slow down and be present.

I tried the Amaro Highball, which immediately stood out for its comfort and balance. It was light yet satisfying, gently effervescent, with bitterness and sweetness in perfect proportion. The finish carried subtle botanical notes rather than harsh alcohol, making it extremely easy to drink and surprisingly soothing.

The Green Shiso Gimlet was equally well-executed. Although the shiso leaf is not visually present, its aroma is clearly expressed, clean, soft, and herbal. Compared to similar cocktails elsewhere, this version feels drier, lighter, and more restrained, with the rice shochu adding clarity and structure.

Overall, Bar GOTO excels in quiet craftsmanship. The cocktails are elegant without being showy, and the space reflects a deep respect for technique and atmosphere. A strong recommendation for those who appreciate subtlety, balance, and Japanese bartending philosophy. ||| [5★ Karen Contreras]: Had a great time at Goto! I had 3 of their good and strong sakura martinis! Place is a but small.Atmosphere was great and customer service was good,they were always checking on me. [4★ Kawone Williams]: Up until 2025 I never heard of Jade and Clover. I figured a visit was in order. So many creative and interesting drinks were left on the menu waiting for me to savor. My guide for the night was the brilliant and at some future point Speed Rack contestant @linet.xx

Here's what I had -

Monkey Buisness- Delicious and texturely complex coconut washed Hendrix Gin combined with Ming River Baijiu for a truly fun cocktail. The monkey elements come from the banana oleo saccharum and banana flambé infused vermouth.👏🏾👏🏾👏🏾

The Jade - This is an absolute banger. I'm not going to pretend I know what red bean orgeat is but I know mixing it with coconut, pandan and lime make for a sense rattling cocktail.The flavor however is nutty and island 🏝 rich. 👏🏾👏🏾👏🏾👏🏾

Thai-rish Tea - Irish whiskey, thai tea, lemon, and condensed milk with just a touch of evaporated milk. The tea is pronounced and that makes this drink slightly dry. The condensed milk is on the inside of the glass and if you want the drink sweeter you give it a stir. Who doesn't love and interactive cocktail. 👏🏾👏🏾👏🏾

Tea No 139 - Wow, this taste like Juicy Fruit chewing gum. A truly simple build with Mijenta tequila infused with oolong tea paired with lemon and demerara syrup. The tea cup is worth the price of entry. 👏🏾👏🏾👏🏾👏🏾

Macau Flip - Vodka, Rum, Sherry, Amaro, vanilla and an egg. What do you get? A coconuty textural explosion of a flip. Don't be afraid of eggs in your drinks. If you've had egg nog you've had eggs in your drink before. You'll be fine and this is delicious 👏🏾👏🏾👏🏾👏🏾

In Love With the Coco - So this was one drink that slightly confused me. Its Ten To One Dark Rum infused with Jackfruit mixed with coconut 🥥 syrup and coconut aminos. What I tasted was a serviceable if not forgettable coconut drink. It felt like there were a few more adventurous places the drink could have gone but it played it safe.👏🏾👏🏾

PB&S - This was the late night sipper. Peanut 🥜 butter washed Rye, black sesame chocolate bitters, and Angostura bitters. Delicious, simple and decadent without being overly sweet. Let’s start with logistics—because apparently those were *optional*. In a three-floor venue, one entire floor with a bathroom was closed. Of the remaining floors, only one had a bathroom. That bathroom was directly next to the coat check line, with zero signage and—more importantly—zero staff managing the chaos.

The result? Two lines melting into one feral social experiment. People waiting for coat check thought they were in the bathroom line, realized they weren’t, and then began cutting, shoving, and pushing the people who had been waiting patiently. Normal venues might step in to de-escalate. Not here.

Instead, the bathroom attendants / bouncers / whoever was on duty decided the correct move was to yell at the people being shoved, rather than the ones doing the shoving. They were rude, aggressive, dismissive, and seemingly annoyed that guests expected basic crowd control on one of the busiest nights of the year.

Coat check was another delight—one single person working, while several other employees stood around doing absolutely nothing. Midnight came and went, and rather than opening the closed bathroom or managing the lines in any functional way, the staff doubled down on hostility.

It takes real talent to ruin New Year’s Eve when you already have music, alcohol, and a crowd ready to have fun—but The Delancey pulled it off. What could have been a great night ended with frustration, shouting, and unnecessary aggression from staff who seemed to resent the very people funding their event.

If you’re considering this venue for a ticketed event: don’t. A pretty theme and good DJ mean nothing if the staff is disorganized, confrontational, and incapable of managing basic human traffic.

Ring in the New Year somewhere else. Trust me.

description: Speakeasy-style bar in the East Village offering exotic cocktails in a cool, hard-to-find space.
google_reviews:
[4★ Peter Gan]: Background
Angel's Share is a legendary speakeasy in New York's East Village, famously hidden behind an unmarked door. It cultivated an almost mythical reputation for classic cocktails and a strict no standing policy long before the modern speakeasy trend peaked.

Ambience
Accessing the bar felt like entering a secret, timeless capsule. The interior was indeed typical of a classic, intimate cocktail den, with dark wood and low lighting. The defining angel mural behind the bar provided a singular touch of whimsical artistry.

Service
Service adhered to the bar's established, slightly formal code. The bartender was skilled and professional, offering solid recommendations with a measured efficiency.

Drink
The cocktail program was dedicated to the classics, executed with reliable precision and quality spirits. The drinks were good, showcasing balance and technique. However, its offerings could feel like a perfectly curated museum display, admirable, but lacking the innovative spark that drives contemporary cocktail culture forward.

Overall
Angel's Share offered a quintessential, refined speakeasy experience that felt like a direct link to a bygone era of New York nightlife. ||| [5★ Jack Tuggle]: I had time to kill on my way to a dinner at Gennisis restaurant and I was walking by this bar and I was like let me try to get in because I could never get in. Beer was cold and the regulars are entertaining. ||| [5★ Marissa Kinler]: Exactly one year overdue for this 5-star review…

I was visiting NYC on a girls trip and, during a very busy Saturday night here, I realized I’d lost a bracelet… the most precious piece of jewelry I own. I was totally devastated, searching the floor, asking the bouncer and bartender every few minutes if anyone had turned it in. After accepting defeat, but before leaving, I scribbled my contact info and a quick drawing of the bracelet on a scrap of receipt paper, hoping for a miracle but knew it was a total shot in the dark. Looking for a small tiny bracelet in a big bar in NYC had the likelihood of finding a needle in a haystack. Even if someone found it, would they even turn it in?

At 3:47 AM, I got a text from manager Jordan saying they’d found my bracelet while cleaning. Like… an actual NYC miracle! I went back the next day, and the bartender on duty returned it with a smile and refused the cash reward I’d offered.

Shoutout to Jordan and the Off the Wagon team….totally honest, kind, incredible humans. They turned what felt like a lost cause into a story I’ll never forget.
